request.question: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?

title: Rick and Morty
HarmonQuest
The Sarah Silverman Program
Harmontown
Great Minds with Dan Harmon
Acceptable.TV
Basic Rocket Science
Community (TV series)
List of Community characters
Introduction to Finality
sentences: Rick and Morty is an American adult animated science-fiction sitcom created by Justin Roiland and Dan Harmon for Cartoon Network's late-night programming block Adult Swim.

The series follows the misadventures of cynical mad scientist Rick Sanchez and his fretful, easily influenced grandson Morty Smith, who split their time between domestic family life and interdimensional adventures.
Roiland voices the series' eponymous characters, with the voice talent of Chris Parnell, Spencer Grammer, and Sarah Chalke providing the rest of the family.
It premiered on December 2, 2013.
HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.
The show is part animated, part live action.
Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.
Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.

The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.

The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.
Harmontown is a weekly live comedy podcast that began airing on June 6, 2012.
It is hosted by writer Dan Harmon, best known as the creator of the sitcom "Community", and actor Jeff B. Davis, best known for his work on the improvisational comedy series "Whose Line Is It Anyway?
" "Harmontown" began as a monthly live comedy show in Los Angeles, California at the NerdMelt Showroom on May 23, 2011, but the show became weekly after Harmon was fired from "Community".
Since the summer of 2012, each episode of "Harmontown" has been recorded and released as a podcast by Feral Audio.
Great Minds with Dan Harmon is an American comedy television series that premiered on February 25, 2016 on the History channel.

The series stars writer Dan Harmon (creator of "Community" and co-creator of "Rick and Morty") and his assistant Spencer Crittenden as themselves, who transport a series of historical figures to the present.
The series is part of History's "Night Class" programming block and was available to stream on History's website and YouTube channel.
The videos have since been removed but short clips are still available on YouTube.
Acceptable.TV is a television program created by Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab that aired on VH1 from March 23 to May 11, 2007.
Each show was composed of several mini-episodes created by the Acceptable.TV staff, with one mini-episode that was submitted by a viewer.
After each episode viewers would be able to vote online for their two favorites.
The two that received the most votes will be continued in the following episode, and the remaining three would be cancelled and replaced by new mini-shows.
The show was adapted from Harmon and Schrab's Channel 101 screenings, and the cast was composed of various Channel 101 contributors.
"Basic Rocket Science" is the fourth episode of the second season of "Community".
It was originally broadcast on October 14, 2010, on NBC.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.

The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.
It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.
Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.
The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.
The show stars:
"Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.
It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.
This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What is the fourth episode in the American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon titled "Community"?
hop1_docs: List of Community characters | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon.  The show, set at the fictional Greendale Community College, depicts the on-campus exploits of students brought together when they form a study group.  The show stars:

Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television | "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" is the series finale of the sitcom "Community", serving as the thirteenth episode of its sixth season.  It was written by series creator Dan Harmon and Chris McKenna, and directed by Rob Schrab.  It is the 110th episode overall and was first released on Yahoo! Screen in the United States on June 2, 2015.

Dan Harmon | Daniel Harmon (born January 3, 1973) is an American writer, producer, and voice actor.  Harmon created and produced the NBC comedy television series "Community", co-created the Adult Swim animated series "Rick and Morty", and co-founded the alternative television network/website Channel 101.  Harmon published "You'll Be Perfect When You're Dead" in 2013.  He also hosts a weekly eponymous podcast, "Harmontown."

Community (TV series) | Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon that aired on NBC and Yahoo! Screen from September 17, 2009 to June 2, 2015.  The series follows an ensemble cast of characters played by Joel McHale, Gillian Jacobs, Danny Pudi, Yvette Nicole Brown, Alison Brie, Donald Glover, Ken Jeong, Chevy Chase, and Jim Rash at a community college in the fictional town of Greendale, Colorado.  It makes heavy use of meta-humor and pop culture references, often parodying film and television clichés and tropes.

Introduction to Finality | "Introduction to Finality" is the 22nd episode of the third season of the American television series "Community" and the third season finale.  It originally aired on May 17, 2012 on NBC.  This was the last episode to air with series creator Dan Harmon as showrunner before he was fired, though Harmon would later return as showrunner for the 5th season.

The Sarah Silverman Program | The Sarah Silverman Program is an American television sitcom, which ran from February 1, 2007 to April 15, 2010 on Comedy Central starring comedian and actress Sarah Silverman, who created the series with Dan Harmon and Rob Schrab.

HarmonQuest | HarmonQuest is an American adult animation/live improvisation television show created by Dan Harmon and Spencer Crittenden.  The show is part animated, part live action.  Dan Harmon and comedians Erin McGathy, Jeff B. Davis, along with game master Spencer Crittenden, perform a fantasy roleplaying campaign in front of a live audience at Victory Studios in Glendale, CA.  Each episode of the show also features a special guest player.  The content of their stories is then animated by Starburns Industries, and the episodes feature a mix of live and animated footage reminiscent of "The Ricky Gervais Show", which Harmon cited as an influence during a Facebook Live tour of Starburns Industries.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Community (show): "Community is an American television sitcom created by Dan Harmon" (passage [4]).
  - Episode mentions found:
    - "Emotional Consequences of Broadcast Television" — "series finale" (passage [2]).
    - "Introduction to Finality" — "22nd episode of the third season" (passage [5]).
  - No passage lists the series episode order or the fourth episode title.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - The question could mean "fourth episode overall" (most likely) or "fourth episode of a particular season" — passages give no episode-order context.
  - No explicit episode list, season-1 guide, or an entry for "Episode 4" is present in the supplied passages.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The exact title of Community's 4th episode in broadcast/official episode order (or clarification if the user meant season-specific fourth episode).

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. "List of Community episodes" (Wikipedia page / episode list).
  2. "Community (season 1) episodes" or "Community season 1 episode list" (season guide).
  3. NBC/official episode guide or "Community episode guide IMDb" for broadcast order.
hop2_docs: List of Unofficial Sentai Akibaranger episodes | This is a list of episodes of the Super Sentai parody series "Unofficial Sentai Akibaranger".  All episode titles in Season 1 use variations of the kanji 痛 generally read as "ita", usually translated as "pain", and is related to the "otaku" phenomenon of the "itasha".  Several episode titles reference past Super Sentai titles.  Examples include episode 2, with the word "Full Blast" referencing "Tokusou Sentai Dekaranger", and episode 3, with the word "Adventure (冒険 , Bōken ) " referencing "GoGo Sentai Boukenger".  The episode titles in "Season Tsuu" have the word "Delusion" (妄想 , Mōsō ) .  The first season aired from April 6 to June 29, 2012 and the second season aired from April 5, 2013 to June 28, 2013.

List of Captain N: The Game Master episodes | This is a list of episodes for the series "".  While the Season 2 and Season 3 episodes were paired with episodes featuring the "Super Mario Bros.", the Captain N episodes themselves retained the title of "Captain N: The Game Master".  Season 3 episodes were half the time length of the previous seasons.

List of McMillan & Wife episodes | This is a list of episodes for the television series "McMillan & Wife".  For the sixth season, the "Wife" was killed off and the title was shortened to "McMillan".  The pilot episode was 120 minutes, seasons 1-2 were 90 minutes episodes, season 3 had both 90 and 120 minutes episodes, seasons 4-5 were 120 minutes episodes and season 6 had 90 minutes episodes.

One of Us (Lost) | "One of Us" is the 16th episode of the third season of "Lost".  It aired on April 11, 2007, making it the 65th episode of the series.  The episode was written by Carlton Cuse and Drew Goddard and directed by Jack Bender.  The title of the episode is not only a reference to the season two episode titled "One of Them", it is also a major theme of the episode, as Jack tries to convince the other flight survivors that Juliet is "one of us", rather than an Other.

Basic Sandwich | "Basic Sandwich" is the thirteenth episode and the season finale of the fifth season of "Community", and the 97th episode overall in the series.  It originally aired on April 17, 2014 on NBC.  The episode was written by Ryan Ridley, making this his series writing debut, and it was directed by Rob Schrab.  The episode is Part 2 of a two-part season finale; Part 1 ("Basic Story") aired on April 10, 2014 on NBC.  The episode initially served as the "de facto" series finale, as "Community" was canceled on May 9, 2014, but on June 30, 2014, series creator Dan Harmon announced that the show was renewed by Yahoo! for a 13-episode sixth season to be streamed online on Yahoo! Screen.

List of Big Windup! episodes | This is a list episodes for the Japanese anime series, "Big Windup! ".  The English titles for the first season are according to the names used by Funimation Entertainment.  English titles in the other seasons are not official.

The Churchills (American band) | The Churchills are an indie pop/rock foursome from the New York/New Jersey area.  They are probably most commonly known for their appearance on the television show Spin City, during season 3 in the episode titled "Internal Affairs".  They performed their song "Everybody Gets What They Deserve" at the end of the show in front of the Flatiron Building in Manhattan.  This same song is also featured in the TV show Scrubs, in the episode titled My Lips Are Sealed.  Meadow Soprano wears a band T-shirt in Episode 3 of Season 2 of The Sopranos, and again in Episode 3 of Season 3.
